content: Use Google Wire for dependency injection to maintain clean architecture, improve testability, and reduce boilerplate code. Wire provides compile-time dependency injection with type safety and no runtime reflection.

Wire (Google): Compile-time DI, type-safe, no reflection, minimal boilerplate
- **Benefits**: Fast, safe, Go-native, perfect for clean architecture
Directory Structure for dependency injection: internal/di/ for providers.go, wire.go, wire_gen.go, alongside domain/, repository/, usecase/, and presentation/.
Provider Pattern: Repository Providers - Provide concrete implementations for repositories like UserRepository, AuthRepository, and TenantRepository.
Provider Pattern: Use Case Providers - Inject dependencies into use cases like AuthenticationUseCase and UserUseCase.
Provider Pattern: Handler Providers - Inject use cases into handlers like AuthHandler and UserHandler.
Wire Sets: Handler Set groups related providers including Repositories, Use Cases, and Handlers.
Wire Sets: Server Set includes HandlerSet, ProvideHTTPServer, and ProvideRouter for server dependencies.
Initialization: Main Application in cmd/main.go should initialize handlers using di.InitializeHandlers(), set up routes, and start the HTTP server.
Wire Generation: In internal/di/wire.go, use `//go:generate wire` and `panic(wire.Build(HandlerSet))` for `InitializeHandlers()` to generate DI code.
Testing Support: Mock Providers - Provide mock implementations for repositories like MockUserRepository and MockAuthRepository, grouped in MockHandlerSet.
Testing Support: Test Initialization - Initialize mock handlers (e.g., di.InitializeMockHandlers()) for testing with mock dependencies.
Configuration: Environment-based Providers - Provide configuration for database and Redis using environment variables.
Principles: Single Responsibility - Each provider has one clear purpose.
Principles: Dependency Declaration - Explicitly declare all dependencies.
Principles: Interface Segregation - Use interfaces for loose coupling.
Principles: Testability - Easy to inject mocks for testing.
Principles: Compile-time Safety - Wire catches DI errors at compile time.
Principles: Minimal Boilerplate - Keep providers simple and focused.
Naming Conventions: Provider Functions should be named `Provide{Type}()` (e.g., `ProvideUserRepository()`).
Naming Conventions: Wire Sets should be named `{Category}Set` (e.g., `HandlerSet`, `ServerSet`).
Naming Conventions: Initialization Functions should be named `Initialize{Category}()` (e.g., `InitializeHandlers()`).
Naming Conventions: Mock Sets should be named `Mock{Category}Set` (e.g., `MockHandlerSet`).
Commands: Generate Wire Code using `go generate ./...` or `wire ./internal/di`.
Commands: Install Wire CLI using `go install github.com/google/wire/cmd/wire@latest` and `go get github.com/google/wire` to add to go.mod.
Benefits: Type Safety - Compile-time verification of dependencies.
Benefits: Performance - No runtime reflection overhead.
Benefits: Maintainability - Clear dependency graph.
Benefits: Testability - Easy to inject mocks.
Benefits: Scalability - Easy to add new dependencies.
Benefits: Clean Architecture - Supports proper layer separation.
Note: Wire generates clean, readable Go code that follows your project's patterns and conventions.
